hooks_by_emotion.description: Hook types organized by the primary emotion they trigger, with intensity ratings and content function mappings

curiosity.description: Triggers the need to know, understand, or discover — creates an open loop the brain must close

examples: The reason most people fail at {X} is...
How I went from {X} to {Y} in {time}...

curiosity.organic_marketing_application: Curiosity hooks are the highest-performing hook emotion for educational content. Use to drive video watch-through and carousel swipes. The open loop must be closed within the content — never clickbait without payoff.

intrigue.description: Deeper than curiosity — suggests hidden, exclusive, or secret knowledge that changes perception

examples: The secret {industry} doesn't want you to know...
After {experience}, I learned what they don't teach...
Everyone says {X}. Here's why they're wrong...

intrigue.organic_marketing_application: Intrigue hooks drive the highest share rates because they make the sharer look 'in the know.' Best for authority positioning content and contrarian takes that position the creator as an insider voice.

surprise.description: Pattern interrupt — violates expectations, forcing attention reallocation
surprise.organic_marketing_application: Surprise is the highest-arousal emotion for sharing (per Berger's research). Visual interrupts are the #1 hook type for short-form video. Myth busters work exceptionally well on LinkedIn and YouTube.

shock.description: Extreme surprise that creates a visceral, involuntary attention response

examples: I {unexpected action} and here's what happened...

shock.organic_marketing_application: Use sparingly — shock hooks create high initial attention but can damage trust if the claim isn't substantiated. Best for transformation content where the proof is in the content itself.

anticipation.description: Forward-looking excitement — the brain simulates the upcoming reward

examples: I was about to give up when...
Something happened yesterday that changed everything...
Three weeks ago I made a decision...

anticipation.organic_marketing_application: Anticipation hooks create the strongest watch-through/read-through rates because the brain is already simulating the reward. Pair with strong payoff content.

engagement.description: Active involvement — pulls the audience into the narrative as participants

hooks.pattern: Start in the middle of action
examples: I was standing in line when...

engagement.organic_marketing_application: In media res is the most powerful story hook — it bypasses the 'should I watch this?' decision by immediately placing the viewer inside a scene. Essential for video content.

empathy.description: Mirror neuron activation — the audience feels what the creator describes

examples: Two years ago I was {bad state}...
Before I figured this out, I was {struggle}...
I know how it feels when {pain}...

empathy.organic_marketing_application: Empathy hooks build the deepest audience connection. They're essential for community content and create 'me too' responses in comments. Best for building superfan relationships per Flynn's model.

trust.description: Credibility and authority signals that reduce skepticism and increase receptivity

examples: After {impressive credential}, here's what I know...
I've {authority builder} and this is what works...
I went from {A} to {B} using this...

trust.organic_marketing_application: Trust hooks convert better than any other emotion for mid-funnel content. Use credential leads sparingly (LinkedIn/YouTube) and results proof more broadly. The time stamp creates specificity that signals truth.

desire.description: Aspiration and wanting — activates the brain's reward anticipation circuits

examples: This is what {goal} actually looks like...

desire.organic_marketing_application: Desire hooks drive saves and bookmarks (people saving content for 'future me'). Pair with value content that delivers on the aspirational promise.

urgency.description: Time pressure and fear of missing out — activates loss aversion

examples: If you're not doing {X}, you're falling behind...
This is costing you more than you think...
You're probably the last to know this...

urgency.organic_marketing_application: Urgency hooks are the most effective for conversion content (Vaynerchuk's 'right hook'). Use loss framing per Kahneman — people feel losses 2x more than gains. But don't overuse — urgency fatigue is real.

belonging.description: Tribal identity activation — 'people like us do things like this' (Godin)

examples: If you're a {identity}, you need to see this...
You know that moment when {relatable situation}?

belonging.organic_marketing_application: Belonging hooks have the highest share rate of any emotion category because sharing IS the identity performance. Per Berger's STEPPS, social currency is the #1 sharing driver. Tribe signal + us vs them create Hanlon's 'pagans/nonbelievers' primal code.

relief.description: The 'finally, a solution' feeling — releases tension the audience didn't know they were holding

examples: Skip {hard thing} and do this instead...

relief.organic_marketing_application: Relief hooks work best when paired with pain point content — first activate the pain, then provide the shortcut. This is the Schwartz 'Solution Aware' level content strategy.

gratitude.description: Reciprocity activation — receiving unexpected value triggers obligation to engage/reciprocate
gratitude.organic_marketing_application: Gratitude hooks activate Cialdini's reciprocity principle. The 'save this' CTA drives the highest save-to-impression ratio, which is a key algorithm signal on Instagram. Use for lead magnet promotion and list building.

reflection.description: Deep thinking activation — forces the audience to pause and consider

examples: What if everything you knew about {X} was wrong?

reflection.organic_marketing_application: Reflection hooks drive the longest comment threads because they invite genuine response. Best for LinkedIn and X where thoughtful engagement signals boost distribution.

defensiveness_then_curiosity.description: Initial resistance ('I don't do that!') that flips to curiosity ('wait, DO I do that?')

examples: If you're still doing {X}, you're leaving money on the table...
This is why your {X} isn't working...

defensiveness_then_curiosity.organic_marketing_application: The two-phase emotional response (defense → curiosity) creates extremely high watch-through rates because people need to see if the criticism applies to them. The most effective hook type for retention metrics.

berger_arousal_mapping.note: Per Berger's research in Contagious: High-arousal emotions drive sharing. Low-arousal emotions (sadness, contentment) suppress it. Content optimized for virality should lead with high-arousal hooks.
